case_metadata.user_request: Find the lowest-energy adsorption configuration for the adsorbate with SMILES [N]=O on the provided surface.
case_metadata.notes: OCD id=909; adsorbate=[N]=O; surface_formula=Mo36N36; y_relaxed=-3.208998 eV; site=8;11;32;35

plan.reasoning: No prior attempts exist, so I should choose the most physically sensible initial adsorption motif for NO on a Mo-containing surface. SMILES consistency check: the adsorbate is [N]=O with atom indices explicitly given as N index 0 and O index 1. N is SP2 with 1 radical electron; O is SP2 with 0 radical electrons. There is no mismatch between the SMILES and the intended adsorbate identity. Because [N]=O is represented here as a radical species, if I use ReactiveSpecies the binding atom should be the atom carrying the unpaired electron, namely N (index 0). For transition-metal surfaces, NO commonly binds through N due to stronger interaction of the N-centered frontier orbital with the metal. The surface offers ontop Mo, bridge Mo-Mo, and hollow motifs involving mostly Mo atoms. As an initial lowest-energy search step, N-bound NO at an ontop Mo site is the most standard and chemically robust starting point; it often serves as the primary chemisorption minimum before testing higher-coordination geometries. I will therefore propose a ReactiveSpecies adsorption through N only at an Mo ontop site. This satisfies the site-alignment rule because ontop uses one adsorbate binding index.

plan.reasoning: SMILES consistency check: the adsorbate is [N]=O, i.e. nitric oxide. The provided atom list shows N at index 0 is SP2 with 1 radical electron, and O at index 1 is SP2 with 0 radical electrons. There is no mismatch between the SMILES and the intended species; this is an open-shell radical. Because I will bind through N(0), which does have an unpaired electron, classifying this as ReactiveSpecies is chemically consistent. History analysis: one successful plan already exists, namely ontop adsorption on Mo via a single Mo atom with energy -10.711 eV. No failures, slips, or bond-change instabilities are reported. Therefore I must propose a completely new plan distinct from the tested ontop(Mo) case. User intent is to find the lowest-energy adsorption configuration of NO on the Mo/N surface, so systematic exploration of stronger multi-center coordination motifs is warranted. Adsorbate analysis: NO commonly binds through N on early transition-metal surfaces, and on nitrides a bridge geometry can outperform ontop due to stronger interaction of the half-filled NO frontier orbital with two metal centers. Since O has no radical electron, a ReactiveSpecies plan should not use O as the binding atom. Surface analysis: available site motifs are ontop Mo, bridge Mo-Mo, and hollow motifs Mo-Mo-N or Mo4. Given the existing strong ontop-Mo result, the most physically sensible new trial is N-bound bridge adsorption across a Mo-Mo pair to test whether two-center metal coordination stabilizes NO further. This is distinct from the previous plan and obeys the allowed canonical site naming. I therefore choose a bridge site with surface_binding_atoms ['Mo','Mo'] and adsorbate_binding_indices [0].

plan.reasoning: SMILES consistency check: the provided adsorbate is [N]=O, i.e. nitric oxide. The RDKit atom list is consistent with this: atom 0 is N (SP2, degree 1, radical_electrons 1), atom 1 is O (SP2, degree 1, radical_electrons 0). There is no mismatch between description and SMILES. Because NO is represented here as a radical species, if I use ReactiveSpecies the binding atom should be the atom that actually carries the unpaired electron, namely N index 0. Analyze old plans: two successful configurations have already been tested. Ontop on Mo with one binding atom gave E = -10.711 eV, which is currently the best. Bridge on Mo-Mo gave E = -10.280 eV, weaker than ontop. No failures or chemical slips are reported, so no site is proven unstable yet. Since I must propose a completely new plan different from prior successful plans, I should not repeat ontop@Mo or bridge@Mo-Mo. Remaining untested physically sensible option is a hollow site. Surface analysis: available hollow environments are Mo-Mo-N and Mo-Mo-Mo-Mo, but per the strict naming rule the site_type must be 'hollow'. For NO on an early transition-metal nitride surface, N-end binding is the most chemically plausible because the radical electron is on N and N usually binds more strongly than O on Mo-containing surfaces. Therefore I will use adsorbate_binding_indices [0]. Among hollow motifs, the all-metal Mo-Mo-Mo-Mo hollow should generally provide stronger metal-adsorbate interaction than the mixed Mo-Mo-N hollow for N-end coordination, so it is the better candidate to test next in the search for a lower energy than the current best ontop value. User intent is to find the lowest-energy adsorption configuration systematically, so continuing to an untested hollow Mo ensemble is appropriate.

plan.reasoning: SMILES consistency check: the adsorbate is [N]=O, i.e. nitric oxide. The provided atom list is consistent with this: N index 0 is SP2 with 1 radical electron, O index 1 is SP2 with 0 radical electrons. There is no mismatch between user request and SMILES. Because I plan to use the radical-bearing N atom as the anchoring atom, ReactiveSpecies is chemically justified: N(0) indeed has an unpaired electron. History analysis: ontop on Mo using one Mo atom succeeded with the best energy so far, E = -10.711 eV. Bridge on Mo-Mo also succeeded but is weaker, E = -10.280 eV. Hollow on Mo-Mo-Mo-Mo underwent rearrangement/geometric slip with bond change count 1, so that hollow-4 environment should be treated as unstable/invalid and avoided. We still need a completely new plan not identical to prior successful or failed plans. The available untested site class is hollow-3 with Mo-Mo-N. Since NO commonly binds through N on transition-metal-rich sites and can also adopt multi-center coordination, the best new physically sensible trial is a hollow site using the radical N atom to probe whether the Mo-rich threefold environment adjacent to N can outperform the tested ontop Mo site. Core intent: systematically search for the lowest-energy adsorption configuration for NO on the Mo/N surface. Adsorbate analysis: NO is a diatomic radical; the chemically preferred primary binding atom on early transition metals is typically N rather than O, especially for strong chemisorption. Surface analysis: allowed site combinations are ontop Mo, bridge Mo-Mo, hollow-3 Mo-Mo-N, hollow-4 Mo-Mo-Mo-Mo. Since ontop and bridge have already been tested and hollow-4 was unstable, the remaining new sensible option is hollow with surface binding atoms ['Mo','Mo','N']. For hollow, one adsorbate binding index is allowed, so I use [0] corresponding exactly to N.

plan.reasoning: SMILES consistency check: the adsorbate is NO with heavy atoms N(index 0) and O(index 1). N is SP2 and carries 1 radical electron; O is SP2 with 0 radical electrons. There is no mismatch between the SMILES and the requested species. Because I plan to use the radical center N(0) as the binding atom, ReactiveSpecies classification is chemically valid. History analysis: tested plans are ontop on Mo with single-point adsorption and it is the best successful result so far at -10.711 eV; bridge on Mo-Mo is weaker at -10.280 eV; hollow on Mo4 rearranged with bond-change count 1, so it is a failure despite moderately low energy; hollow on Mo-Mo-N is explicitly unstable and slipped to ontop, so Mo-Mo-N hollow must be forbidden. Since the task requires a completely new plan and we must continue systematic exploration, the remaining sensible unexplored option is to vary the adsorption orientation/mode rather than repeat the exact prior single-point N-ontop motif. For NO on early transition-metal Mo, a physically reasonable alternative is side-on adsorption engaging both N and O across a Mo-Mo bridge. This is distinct from the previous bridge attempt if that prior attempt used only one adsorbate binding point; here I explicitly request two adsorbate binding indices [0,1] to enforce side-on coordination. This can sometimes outperform end-on motifs on nitride/metal surfaces by better back-donation into the NO pi* manifold, so it is worth testing within the remaining attempts. User core intent: identify the lowest-energy adsorption configuration for NO on the given Mo/N surface. Surface analysis: available valid site families are ontop Mo, bridge Mo-Mo, hollow Mo-Mo-N, hollow Mo-Mo-Mo-Mo; Mo-Mo-N hollow is forbidden from history, and the Mo4 hollow already failed by rearrangement. Therefore bridge Mo-Mo with side-on NO is the best new physically sensible plan.
